subject: active timeline audio topology
claim: The timeline currently served by the running desktop app does contain a full-length detached audio producer, while the paired picture producer explicitly disables its embedded audio.
evidence_quote: picture producer: audio_index=-1 and astream=-1 (lines 12-19); mock-audio uses the same .mov with video_index=-1 and vstream=-1 (lines 100-104); playlist3 is marked shotcut:audio=1 and mounted hide=video (lines 140-153).
impact: The symptom is not explained by a missing audio track or by accidental double playback of embedded and detached sound.
implication: The causal search should continue downstream through resolveAudio, decoding, Web Audio scheduling, and the WKWebView output device.
notes: The running native sidecar on loopback reported repo=/Users/tejas/Github/vean/projects/carlo-demo and defaultRoute=timeline:main; /api/timeline resolved to this file.

subject: source media signal
claim: The detached source has a valid 48 kHz mono AAC stream with nonzero signal, but it is intrinsically quiet: whole-file mean -37.6 dBFS and max -16.1 dBFS; the first five seconds average -41.0 dBFS and peak at -20.4 dBFS.
evidence_quote: ffprobe: aac, 48000 Hz, mono, duration 131.754667 s; ffmpeg volumedetect: mean_volume -37.6 dB, max_volume -16.1 dB; first 5 s mean_volume -41.0 dB, max_volume -20.4 dB.
impact: The bytes are not silent, but low playback gain can make an otherwise functioning route seem silent on modest speaker volume.
implication: Low source level is a ranked contributing cause, not a complete explanation; output-level metering or normalization would make this distinction visible.
notes: Ranked hypothesis #2 for perceived silence. The media file was taken directly from the active timeline reference, not discovered by a filesystem search.

subject: resolveAudio stream selection
claim: resolveAudio honors MLT stream-disable selectors and treats hide=video on an audio track as an audio-track marker, so the active document resolves the detached mock-audio clip and skips mock-picture audio.
evidence_quote: clipDecodesAudio returns false for astream=-1 or audioIndex=-1 (lines 214-219); audio tracks are always walked despite hidden (lines 359-370); video tracks are also walked but their disabled clips are skipped (lines 371-381).
impact: IR-to-schedule selection is consistent with the intended detached A/V topology.
implication: A resolver bug is ruled out for this active timeline; the resolved schedule should contain exactly one audible source.

subject: preview audio ownership
claim: Program-monitor sound is not emitted by an HTMLMediaElement; the app fetches each original resource, calls AudioContext.decodeAudioData, schedules AudioBufferSourceNodes, and connects the master GainNode directly to AudioContext.destination.
evidence_quote: constructor creates AudioContext and masterGain.connect(ctx.destination) (lines 96-111); loadBuffer fetches /api/media and decodeAudioData(arrayBuffer) (lines 192-220); scheduleClip creates and starts AudioBufferSourceNode through clip and track gains (lines 275-345).
impact: Native-media-element playback success does not prove program-monitor sound, and WKWebView Web Audio behavior is the relevant platform seam.
implication: The most likely remaining failure region is decodeAudioData or AudioContext.destination in the actual WKWebView, not the canvas/video compositor.

subject: live Chromium product path
claim: A fresh headless Chromium session against the same carlo-demo loopback server reached a running AudioContext, buffered one resource, scheduled one clip at unity master gain, and produced nonzero PCM at the product master GainNode.
evidence_quote: before: master GainNode=1; after Play: contextState=running, scheduledClips=1, bufferedResources=1, playing=true; analyser samples reached max RMS 0.0190208 and max peak 0.0513521.
impact: Source fetch, browser decode, active-timeline resolution, scheduling, gain, and graph signal are proven through the last product node before the browser output destination in Chromium.
implication: Ranked hypothesis #1 is a WKWebView/CoreAudio destination or native-context-state problem, because the same HTTP frontend and source produce nonzero master-bus PCM in Chromium.
notes: This does not prove physical speaker output because headless Chromium uses a different engine/output host than WKWebView.

subject: system audio routing
claim: macOS reports output volume 61, output muted=false, and Razer Nommo Chroma as both the default output and default system output at 48 kHz.
evidence_quote: osascript volume settings: output volume:61, output muted:false; system_profiler SPAudioDataType: Razer Nommo Chroma, Default Output Device: Yes, Default System Output Device: Yes, 48000 Hz.
impact: A globally muted or zero-volume Mac is ruled out, but a physically unavailable or independently muted USB speaker remains possible.
implication: The app's system-default route should target the Razer device; a native output meter or explicit device identity in diagnostics is needed to distinguish browser routing from hardware state.
notes: Read-only inspection; no system audio setting was changed.

subject: whole-file audio decode
claim: The program monitor downloads and decodes the entire original 188,243,358-byte H.264/AAC .mov solely to obtain audio, rather than streaming an audio element or consuming an audio-only proxy.
evidence_quote: loadBuffer performs fetch(url), await res.arrayBuffer(), then ctx.decodeAudioData(bytes) and caches by resource (lines 197-220). The active source is 188,243,358 bytes for 131.87 seconds.
impact: WKWebView must allocate the full container plus decoded PCM before any source can be scheduled; failure or memory pressure produces apparent silence.
implication: Ranked hypothesis #3 is WKWebView-specific full-.mov decode failure or latency, especially for longer camera files; an extracted audio proxy or streaming decoder would avoid this systemic cliff.

subject: silent failure telemetry
claim: Every fetch/decode failure is converted to null and silently skipped, sink-routing errors are swallowed, and AudioGraphStats exposes neither failures nor master volume/mute/sink state.
evidence_quote: loadBuffer catch returns null (lines 202-217); setSinkId catches and ignores errors (lines 142-151); getStats returns only context/sample/time/counts/playing (lines 420-429).
impact: The UI and existing bridge can say playing with zero sound without explaining whether the cause is fetch, decode, autoplay, gain, sink selection, or destination output.
implication: This is the main diagnostic blocker: the native symptom cannot be causally attributed from current product state even though the graph has a debug bridge.
notes: Blind spot #1. The smallest diagnostic next action is to surface per-resource load state/error, effective master gain, requested/applied sink, and context state transitions.

subject: audio output selection
claim: The output menu is enabled by testing HTMLMediaElement.prototype.setSinkId even though playback is routed through AudioContext.setSinkId, and AudioGraph records the requested sink even when application fails.
evidence_quote: SINK_SUPPORTED checks HTMLMediaElement.prototype (lines 25-26); AudioGraph separately feature-tests ctx.setSinkId, catches failure, but keeps this.sinkId (audioGraph.ts lines 142-155).
impact: On engines where media-element and AudioContext sink capabilities differ, the UI can show a selected device that the actual graph never applied.
implication: This is a systemic routing defect for non-default outputs and a plausible cause if the native session selected a device, though the current fresh state defaults to system output.
notes: Ranked hypothesis #4 for this incident because App initializes sinkId to the empty system-default value.

subject: autoplay and context lifecycle
claim: The gesture unlock listener permanently marks itself unlocked before knowing whether resume succeeded, while AudioGraph only calls resume for state=suspended and suppresses resume errors.
evidence_quote: unlock sets unlocked=true then void audioGraph.current?.resume() (FootageStage lines 668-674); resume catches and returns on rejection and only branches for exact state suspended (audioGraph.ts lines 161-174).
impact: A rejected, interrupted, or otherwise non-running WKWebView context can remain silent without retry feedback; graph.play later also suppresses the same failure.
implication: Ranked hypothesis #5: inspect actual WKWebView context state and statechange events during a real click before changing autoplay logic.
notes: The active Chromium loopback did resume successfully, so this is platform-specific and not demonstrated in the native session.

subject: asynchronous decode timing
claim: When play begins before preload finishes, schedule captures timelineNow once before awaiting decode, then starts the source from that stale offset after loading completes.
evidence_quote: schedule computes nowSec once and launches async scheduleClip (lines 248-267); scheduleClip awaits loadBuffer before deriving ctxNow and sourceOffset from the old nowSec (lines 275-332).
impact: Large-source decode latency causes an initial silent gap followed by late, out-of-sync audio from the beginning rather than audio aligned to the current playhead.
implication: This can turn the 188 MB full-container decode into a user-visible no-sound interval even when decode eventually succeeds; scheduling should recompute the playhead after await.
notes: Ranked hypothesis #3b. The live Chromium resource transfer alone took about 0.67 s; WKWebView decode time is unmeasured.

subject: development desktop resource duplication
claim: In the current tauri dev app, React StrictMode recreates the graph while loadBuffer has no AbortController, so the active 188 MB audio source is fetched twice during startup.
evidence_quote: App is wrapped in StrictMode (main.tsx lines 11-16); graph cleanup clears maps and closes the context but cannot abort fetches already started (audioGraph.ts lines 197-220, 432-455). Live resource timing showed two 188,243,658-byte /api/media transfers beginning about 16 ms apart.
impact: The development desktop path can consume roughly 376 MB of transfer plus duplicate decode work before playback, amplifying WKWebView memory/latency failure risk.
implication: Even if not the sole root cause, in-flight fetch cancellation is a systemic correctness and stability requirement for audio graph teardown.
notes: StrictMode effect replay is development-only, matching the currently running target/debug/vean-app workflow rather than a release bundle.

subject: native codec coverage
claim: The actual WKWebView harness proves decodeAudioData for standalone WAV, M4A AAC-LC, and MP3 fixtures, but it does not decode an AAC stream from a camera .mov through AudioGraph or prove signal reaches AudioContext.destination.
evidence_quote: audio() fetches a static fixture and measures decoded AudioBuffer RMS (lines 93-108); cells cover audio-pcm.wav, audio-aac.m4a, and audio.mp3 (lines 222-235). No product transport, AudioBufferSourceNode, destination analyser, sink, or .mov audio cell is exercised.
impact: Existing native codec evidence cannot rule out the leading WKWebView full-.mov/product-graph hypotheses.
implication: Blind spot #2: add an actual WKWebView product-path cell using the active container class and assert nonzero post-master signal plus context/sink state.

subject: browser completion oracle
claim: The canonical browser playback assertion requires only playing=true and contextState=running, then compares clock times; it never asserts scheduledClips, bufferedResources, effective gain, or nonzero output samples.
evidence_quote: waitForFunction checks __veanAudio.playing and contextState only (lines 182-188); the following samples capture master_frame and context_time only (lines 189-208).
impact: A running but silent graph satisfies the browser oracle, exactly matching the reported failure shape.
implication: Blind spot #3: add post-master analyser evidence and a negative control so silence cannot pass as successful playback.

subject: causal synthesis
claim: Evidence localizes the incident downstream of active-document resolution and upstream Chromium mixing: the leading cause is WKWebView destination/context/routing behavior; low source level and whole-.mov decode latency/failure are bounded competitors, while missing track, muted Mac, and resolver selection are ruled out.
evidence_quote: Rank: (1) actual WKWebView context/destination/sink state, medium-high; (2) source level around -38 to -41 dBFS mean, medium; (3) 188 MB whole-container decode plus stale-offset start and dev duplicate fetch, medium; (4) explicit non-default sink mismatch, low for fresh default state; (5) missing/disabled document audio, ruled out.
impact: A code-only fix chosen before native state is captured risks treating the wrong layer and preserving silent failure modes.
implication: Smallest correct next action: run the existing embedded WKWebView provider against a detached AAC-in-.mov product timeline and record load errors, context state transitions, effective gain, applied sink, and post-master RMS; then fix the observed failing boundary.
notes: Primary blocker: the active non-instrumented target/debug app exposes no safe attachment surface for reading its WKWebView AudioContext, and current __veanAudio stats omit the needed failure/gain/sink fields.
